data.title: Raymond James Financial: One Of My Best Ideas In The Financials Space
data.description:
Raymond James Financial Inc. (RJF) is an American multinational independent investment bank and financial services company. Founded in 1962, Raymond James is now a $29 billion (by market cap) financial services mover and shaker employing nearly 20,000 people. The company reports results across four segments: Private Client Group, 68% of FY 2025 revenue; Capital Markets, 12%; Bank, 12%; Asset Management, 8%.

Raymond James mainly creates revenue (via PCG) by providing financial planning, investment advisory, and securities transaction services through a large and developed branch office network throughout the US, Canada, and the U.K. In doing so, and by offering a one-stop shop for wealthy clients through its full suite, Raymond James generates asset management and administration fees after acquiring fee-based accounts and placing them under administration.

Raymond James has approximately $1.8 trillion in assets under administration, which represents the underlying fee and compounding engine for the entire enterprise. And this engine is expanding from two powerful forces working simultaneously. First, there’s the nature of global capital markets, leading to higher asset prices over time (which lifts market values of AUA and then the fees applied to AUA). Second, there’s the consistent net inflows of assets, increasing AUA yet again (and, thus, fees).

While Raymond James has no control over the first component, that second component is where the company shines. Whereas a lot of legacy asset managers have struggled with persistent net outflows, Raymond James continues to attract assets via its relationship-heavy, client-first, advisor-centric model via a wide branch network. The company recorded $52 billion in domestic PCG net new assets for FY 2025, and these assets tend to be sticky once in place. These two forces are showing no signs of abating, indicating that Raymond James has plenty of ability to continue growing its revenue, profit, and dividend over the years ahead.

Dividend Growth, Growth Rate, Payout Ratio and Yield

The company has already increased its dividend for 14 consecutive years. Its 10-year dividend growth rate of 15.3% is impressive, but what might be more impressive is the consistency – Raymond James consistently hands out double-digit dividend raises (often in a low-teens to mid-teens range). However, when you’re getting this kind of dividend growth, the common trade-off one has to make is yield.

Unsurprisingly, that trade-off shows up in the stock’s lowish starting yield of 1.4%. This yield is 10 basis points higher than its own five-year average, so there’s some solace there, but this kind of setup is probably more suitable for those with a long-term compounding mindset. With a payout ratio of just 20.3%, that long-term dividend snowball compounding process still has a long hill in front of it. For younger dividend growth investors who have the time and patience for the compounding process to unfold and work its magic, this dividend profile looks terrific.

Revenue and Earnings Growth

As terrific as it may be, though, much of this profile is built using past information. However, investors must always be thinking about the future, as today’s capital ultimately gets risked for tomorrow’s rewards. As such, I’ll now build out a forward-looking growth trajectory for the business, which will be useful when the time comes later to estimate intrinsic value.

I’ll first show you what the business has done over the last ten years in terms of its top-line and bottom-line growth. I’ll then reveal a professional prognostication for near-term profit growth. Lining up the proven past with a future forecast in this manner should give us the ability to roughly gauge where the business could be going from here.

Raymond James moved its revenue from $5.5 billion in FY 2016 to $15.9 billion in FY 2025. That’s a compound annual growth rate of 12.5%. Excellent. I usually look for a mid-single-digit (or better) top-line growth rate out of a fairly mature company like this, but Raymond James is knocking it out of the park. Meanwhile, earnings per share grew from $2.43 to $10.30 over this period, which is a CAGR of 17.4%.

Very strong bottom-line growth here, aided by expanding margins and steady buybacks. What’s really incredible here is that the bottom-line growth was secular in nature, with only YOY decline over the last decade (which occurred in FY 2020 during the depths of the pandemic). I must say, I’m a bit stunned by the consistent operational excellence.

Looking forward, CFRA believes that Raymond James will compound its EPS at an annual rate of 10% over the next three years. I’m inclined to believe this is closer to a floor than a ceiling for Raymond James. A 10% hurdle shouldn’t be hard to clear for a company that did north of 17% over the last decade.

CFRA highlights the company’s high-quality nature, complementary suite, numerous tailwinds gusting its way (e.g., fee gathering, rising capital markets), disciplined M&A (e.g., Clark Capital), and the employment of AI via the proprietary AI agent “Rai” (which is being used by 10,000 associates). Raymond James has nearly perfected its client-first, advisor-centric model that leads to entrenched, sticky relationships, and using AI and strategic acquisitions to steadily increase its network and scale makes it nearly unbeatable.

With a 10% EPS growth forecast on the table, that positions the dividend for low-teens (or better) growth over the coming years (by virtue of the low payout ratio). Pairing that with the starting yield gets one to a mid-teens type of annualized total return – before any kind of multiple expansion. It’s one of the more appealing setups I’ve come across recently.

Financial Position

Moving over to the balance sheet, Raymond James has an outstanding financial position. Its long-term debt/equity ratio is 0.3. As good as that is, it belies the company’s true financial strength. I say that because Raymond James is sitting on net cash. The company’s credit ratings, which are at the higher end of investment-grade territory, further show what a strong institution this is: A-, Fitch; A3, Moody’s; and A-, S&P.

Profitability is robust. Return on equity has averaged 18% over the last five years, while net margin has averaged 15.1%. Fat margins, high returns on capital. From top to bottom, this is a very high-quality business. And with economies of scale, “sticky” assets, switching costs, a wide branch network, brand recognition in the industry (both for advisors and clients), financial expertise, and its one-stop shop full suite, the company does benefit from durable competitive advantages.

Of course, there are risks to consider. Regulation, litigation, and competition are omnipresent risks in every industry. I view this industry as especially regulated and competitive. The secular shift toward passive funds is a serious pressure point (i.e., fee compression), although the company’s standing as a wider wealth management firm helps to mitigate this.

Raymond James is directly exposed to volatility across global capital markets, as well as macroeconomics, interest rates, and broader economic cycles. The company also has exposure to geopolitics and currency exchange rates. Raymond James must continue to attract advisors and clients in order to support AUM growth. While I think it’s important to acknowledge the risks, the quality of the firm deserves acknowledgment.

And so does the valuation of the business…

Valuation

The stock is trading hands for a P/E ratio of 15.3. That’s about half that of the broader market’s earnings multiple. When you line this up against the EPS growth, we’re talking about a PEG ratio below 1. The P/S ratio is barely above 2. The P/CF ratio is less than 13. And the yield, as noted earlier, is slightly higher than its own recent historical average. So the stock looks cheap when looking at basic valuation metrics. But how cheap might it be? What would a rational estimate of intrinsic value look like?

I valued shares using a two-stage dividend discount model analysis. I factored in a 10% discount rate, a 10-year dividend growth rate of 14%, and a long-term dividend growth rate of 8%. I’m basically extrapolating the prior decade of demonstrated dividend growth into the next decade (albeit with a minor adjustment downward on a go-forward basis). This modestly conservative extrapolation doesn’t seem unreasonable when we see how low the payout ratio is, how fast the business is growing (and is expected to continue growing), and how strong the balance sheet is. I see this as a pretty realistic set of assumptions to make for the business.

The DDM analysis gives me a fair value of $193.14. The reason I use a dividend discount model analysis is because a business is ultimately equal to the sum of all the future cash flow it can provide. The DDM analysis is a tailored version of the discounted cash flow model analysis, as it simply substitutes dividends and dividend growth for cash flow and growth.

It then discounts those future dividends back to the present day to account for the time value of money, since a dollar tomorrow is not worth the same amount as a dollar today. I find it to be a fairly accurate way to value dividend growth stocks. I’m seeing quite a bit of positive daylight between price and value here.

But we’ll now compare that valuation with where two professional stock analysis firms have come out at. This adds balance, depth, and perspective to our conclusion.

Morningstar, a leading and well-respected stock analysis firm, rates stocks on a 5-star system. 1 star would mean a stock is substantially overvalued; 5 stars would mean a stock is substantially undervalued. 3 stars would indicate roughly fair value. Morningstar rates RJF as a 3-star stock, with a fair value estimate of $180.00.

CFRA is another professional analysis firm, and I like to compare my valuation opinion to theirs to see if I’m out of line. They similarly rate stocks on a 1-5 star scale, with 1 star meaning a stock is a strong sell and 5 stars meaning a stock is a strong buy. 3 stars is a hold. CFRA rates RJF as a 3-star “HOLD”, with a 12-month target price of $180.00.

Morningstar and CFRA are in perfect alignment. I’m not that far off. Averaging the three numbers out gives us a final valuation of $184.38, which would indicate the stock is possibly 16% undervalued.

Bottom line: Raymond James Financial Inc. is a high-quality investment bank that has perfected its advisor-centric, client-first model, bolstered by its wide branch network and full suite of complementary capabilities. AUM is rising both organically and via the power of compounding, meaning the firm almost cannot lose over time. With a market-beating yield, double-digit dividend growth, a very low payout ratio, nearly 15 consecutive years of dividend increases, and the potential that shares are 16% undervalued, this might just be one of my best ideas in the financials space for long-term dividend growth investors who aren’t in dire need of immediate income.

Note from D&I: How safe is RJF's dividend? We ran the stock through Simply Safe Dividends, and as we go to press, its Dividend Safety Score is 99. Dividend Safety Scores range from 0 to 100. A score of 50 is average, 75 or higher is excellent, and 25 or lower is weak. With this in mind, RJF’s dividend appears Very Safe with a very unlikely risk of being cut.

Disclosure: I have no position in RJF.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Despite Its Name, The Defiance Quantum ETF Is NOT A Bona Fide Quantum Computing ETF
data.description:
Sometimes, I really hate lawyers!

And that says a lot coming from me. I got admitted to the bar in New York and in local federal courts back in 1977. And when I’m not writing for Seeking Alpha, I’m still doing things for which I can and do put Esq. after my name.

My legal endeavors remain very much secondary to investment analysis. Even so, I’m not averse to coming up with some out-of-the-box legal arguments. I’m scheduled to make an especially bold one in court very soon, on 4/24/26.

But I never have and never will run afoul of ethical rules that could penalize lawyers for making so-called frivolous arguments. These refer to arguments that have no basis in current law — unless we make a credible case urging the court to change the law. (Yes, there are many ways courts can actually do this.)

I cannot apply the frivolous label to the prospectus and supporting documents though which the Defiance Quantum ETF (QTUM) came into and remains in existence. The standard for such things is factual accuracy. QTUM’s documents satisfy that. So, I’ll tip my hat to the Esquires who allowed this ETF to see the light of day.

But we, the Seeking Alpha community, are not in a court of law. We are part of the investment community. Here, I believe, we have a tougher standard!

For us, strict factual accuracy is not good enough. We need things to make sense from an investment standpoint. So, the question for us is…

Does QTUM provide reasonable exposure to the potential rewards and risks we expect from investment in quantum computing?

This is not a simple question of black-letter law. It’s a matter of opinion based on investment common sense. And in this regard, I’ll say QTUM fails badly.

Where’s The Quantum?

ETF portfolios are matters of public record.

The top holdings for QTUM are presented here on Seeking Alpha.

The three main quantum computing stocks are D-Wave Quantum (QBTS), IonQ (IONQ), and Rigetti (RGTI).

Do you see any of them in Seeking Alpha’s presentation of the fund’s top holdings?

There’s nothing wrong with your eyes.

Those stocks remain absent from this table.

Quantum Computing (QUBT) is a more intriguing case. Personally, I see it as more of a photonics play. In the past, I referred to it as quantum adjacent. Ultimately, though, I’d have no objection to including it in a list of quantum computing stocks. It, too, is absent from Seeking Alpha’s presentation of top holdings.

There are some other companies whose names seem to suggest quantum computing. I’m referring to Arqit Quantum (ARQQ) and Quantum eMotion (QNC).

I haven’t written about either of these. I looked into them several times because of their names. I wanted to make sure I wasn’t missing anything in my efforts to fully cover the group. It seems to me, though, that they are mainly about cyber-security.

For the record, neither of those are among QTUM’s top holdings.

I’d be remiss if I omitted mention of Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and IBM (IBM).

Both are involved in quantum computing. But good luck finding anything in the financial statements of either that suggests quantum computing exposure amounts to more than a rounding error. And — neither GOOGL nor IBM are among QTUM’s top ten holdings.

Here are the current percentage stakes. (Note: The percentages below, as of this writing, are more recent than the Seeking Alpha 3/31/26 as-of date.)

So, what’s going on here? How is this possible,

How can an ETF that names itself as a quantum computing fund have such an overwhelming majority of its portfolio invested in stocks whose investment (return-risk) characteristics have nothing to do with quantum computing?

Notice I emphasized the phrase “investment (return-risk) characteristics.”

That’s the lawyer in me fighting logic with logic. I’m depriving Defiance and/or its legal team of an opportunity to object to this write-up and tell me to carefully read the prospectus!

Speaking of which, let’s now turn to that document and examine…

The QTUM Pseudo-Secret Sauce

Here I go again — playing lawyer.

Nothing in the prospectus is really secret. Having been filed with the S.E.C., it’s a matter of public record. And Defiance makes it one-click accessible every day from the ETF’s home page.

I use the word “pseudo” because rather than being a literal secret, the QTUM investing strategy is a fake or imitation secret. I say that because of a belief that many, perhaps most, investors don’t read and carefully think about what’s said in ETF prospectuses and related documents.

In other words, QTUM is not keeping it a secret. Instead, I believe many in the investment community choose to keep it secret from themselves by ignoring it, or skimming too casually.

Am I being too cynical? There is no objective answer to that. The best I can do is give you the ammunition to dispute and discard my opinion if you so choose.

So let’s get to it.

The prospectus doesn’t actually tell us how QTUM picks its investments. Instead, page 3 of the portion of this multi-ETF document that pertains to QTUM tells us the QTUM ETF tracks the Blue Star Machine Learning and Quantum Computing Index.

A paragraph follows containing a verbose summary of that index. But let’s skip that and go instead to the first-hand source, the Index Methodology document itself which, to Defiance’s credit, is also easily linked to from the QTUM home page.

That document has a lot of mind-numbing detail that isn’t really pertinent to what we now want to know. But the Table of Contents points us to our goal, “Pure Play/Thematic Screening” on page 3.

Going there, we find what we’re looking for. Here it is verbatim:

The index includes companies whose business activity, products, or services include one of the following in relation to the development or commercialization of quantum computing or machine learning technology, based principally on companies’ most recent annual filing:

• Quantum Computing, including:

– super conducting materials,

– applications or algorithms built for quantum computers, and/or

– equipment and materials used specifically in the manufacturing of quantum computers.

• Machine Learning, including:

– advanced computing hardware (such as GPUs, FPGAs, ASICs, and other embedded artificial intelligence chips),

– semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and/or

– companies that specialize in the perception, collection, visualization, or management of big data.

The index is rebalanced semi-annually, and is more or less equally weighted. (Page 7 of the Methodology document describes the details.)

So now you know. The ETF legally opens its doors to companies other than QBTS, IONQ, RGTI, and QUBT.

Come to think of it, the fund has no choice.

The combined market capitalization of those four quantum stocks is about $34 billion. It would be unrealistic to launch an ETF entirely from such a limited field.

So the idea of adding stocks that, so to speak, ride the coattails of quantum computing is a necessary bow to practical reality.

The question is how reasonable this is.

Looking For Investment Logic

I encourage you to stop heading now and go back to the index methodology language.

Read it again. Think about it.

Now, go to the QTUM home page and access the complete portfolio.

Look at the names. Think about them, or at least some of them in connection with the prospectus methodology.

Then, come back here and continue reading.

I’ll bet that on a quick skim of the portfolio, it comes off as a reasonable expression of what the methodology document seeks.

Now, I’ll turn to a favorite saying of the late Richard Farrell, a long-time Professor at Brooklyn Law School. In response to student comments that struck him as off-base, he’d smilingly say, “That sounds good if you say it fast enough.”

Borrowing from Professor Farrell, I’ll now suggest that good feelings you might have about the portfolio probably seem good if you skim fast enough.

Let’s slow down and consider some specific companies.

How about RadNet (RDNT)? That company does outpatient radiology imaging. How are the investment characteristics of that stock related in any way to quantum computing? RDNT’s lawyers don’t seem to think so. Retrieve its latest 10K and search for the word quantum. You’ll come up with zero.

Now, general quantum computing rhetoric mentions healthcare as a field that supposedly will benefit from quantum computing. But how and why RDNT in particular? I might have suspected pharmaceutical or biotech research — i.e. for optimization tasks in which QBTS specializes. But your friendly neighborhood radiology place? Really?

Now, there is talk of RDNT using AI. And the QTUM index document casts a wide net by also saying ML (machine learning).

But AI and ML are not synonymous. Amazon Web Services tells us:

Machine learning (ML) is a specific branch of artificial intelligence (AI). ML has a limited scope and focus compared to AI. AI includes several strategies and technologies that are outside the scope of machine learning.

So can RDNT even fit into QTUM’s ML mandate? I don’t know.

More to the point, QTUM identifies itself, by its name, as quantum computing.

It would seem to take an extreme stretching of legal logic to make RDNT a proper QTUM holding. First, you’d have to stretch to assume its approach to AI encompasses the ML subset. And then, you have to accept that the lawyers provided cover for the fund’s failure to include ML (or even AI) in its name by at last including them in the formal documents you may or may not ever access or read.

How happy are you putting real money into an ETF that thinks this way? Yes, it’s legal. But is it good? You decide!

Let’s shift gears—even though I’ve never actually seen a real-life quantum computer, I’ll bet they need a lot of memory. So I could see them equipped with memory made by Micron Technology (MU).

Actually, I don’t know if I have this right. Would the type of server-oriented memory sold by MU really go into a specific quantum computer? Can the server approach really apply to quantum computing?

But here are things I do know. MU’s investment characteristics are being described in terms of whether the memory cycle is peaking or still in an AI-inspired secular upturn. I also know that the word “quantum” does not appear anywhere in MU's latest 10K. (How great is Ctrl+F, quick Find!)

I could go on and on and on — but I think you get the message.

We may have here the next great party game, right up there with charades and Trivial Pursuit. Print out sets of the QTUM portfolio. Then, have participants compete to find companies that really belong in a so-called Quantum ETF. Enjoy!

But I suggest that no amount of party gaming or investment common-sense assessment of reward and risk can make 2.85% (or 6.79% if you want to stretch to define things more broadly, or 6.78% if, as the table suggests, we limit decimal rounding to two places rather than the larger number Excel used) of the QTUM portfolio a truly legitimate play on quantum computing.

We can potentially get there if we focus on ML—assuming we carefully distinguish between AI and the smaller ML subset.

Legally speaking, QTUM is fine. Its prospectus essentially incorporates by reference a separate index document that makes ample logical reference to ML.

But the name of this ETF, and the appeal it presents as a quantum computing theme are, I think, way out of line.

I’d probably think better of it if it completely abandoned all references to quantum computing and renamed itself Defiance AI/ML ETF, or something like that. We’d be able to identify a larger number of similar ETFs against which we’d compare it. But that’s life!

An Alternative Quantum Computing ETF

If you want an ETF that really offers you a play on quantum computing in the context of a risk-reducing more diversified portfolio, consider the WisdomTree Quantum Computing ETF (WQTM).

Here’s its portfolio, explanation of quantum, investment case, and prospectus. Note: Wisdom Tree makes you hunt really hard for these links. Yuk! Phooey!)

Here’s the methodology document for the index WQTM tracks. (See pages 171-77.)

Here’s the WQTM eligibility rhetoric:

Component companies must be involved in Quantum Computing Activities. Based on WisdomTree’s assessment, the business activities listed below currently are reflective of the Quantum Computing space (“Quantum Computing Activities”). The list below is not exhaustive and may be modified from time to time based on WisdomTree’s or the Committee’s evaluation of the Quantum Computing space and related information

  1. Quantum chips and qubit technology providers,
  2. Quantum software and algorithm providers,
  3. Quantum annealing and simulation providers,
  4. Quantum-as-a-Service providers,
  5. Post-quantum cryptography providers,
  6. Quantum networking and communications providers,
  7. Advanced computing providers,
  8. Providers of tools and infrastructure, as well as semiconductors, materials, and components to companies involved in the development of Quantum computing technologies.

* * * *

The Committee assigns each component company a Relevancy Score and a Purity Classification based on the nature and significance of its Quantum Computing Activities, and, where applicable, revenue derived from those activities.

This is interesting. As with QTUM, there’s a lot of room for open-ended wandering. But…

WQTM’s use of relevancy scores is interesting.

This sounds subjective. But for a thematic ETF, that’s probably necessary — and a good thing. It forces WQTM’s index team to think carefully (and possibly have some bold debates in-house). It seems like such discipline could limit the kind of sprawling we saw with QTUM.

WQTM’s top holdings, as presented on Seeking Alpha make it clear, even at a hasty glance, that this ETF is better designed to give investors legitimate exposure to quantum computing.

Its top four positions, amounting to 23.26% of the portfolio (as of the present, not the Seeking Alpha 3/31/26 as-of date), consist of the exact four we expect to see, RGTI, IONQ, QBTS, and QUBT.

Here’s my exposure breakdown:

You can see the complete portfolio by going here, scrolling down to Holdings, clicking the link that says “View All Holdings,” and then, in the popup screen, clicking on the link that says “Export Holdings.” (Oh my goodness — I hate having to travel the multi-step path Wisdom Tree puts me on. Again I sat, yuk, phooey! But at least this portfolio makes more sense.)

Ultimately, WQTM, like QUTM, invests mainly in non-quantum stocks. But like QUTM, it has no choice. There’s just so much money any ETF can invest in quantum specifically.

So we’ll always need expanded logic to create an ETF. But in my opinion, WQTM does it better.

Note, though, that…

WQTM Is Very Tiny

I must point out, however, that WQTM is miniature. Assets under management here amount to about $40 million (that’s million, not billion).

This may suggest to some a potential liquidity problem, as happens when we deal with very small stocks and market capitalizations.

But that is not so for ETFs.

With ETFs, what counts is the liquidity of the stocks in the portfolio, not the amount of assets held in the ETF. Even the tiniest ETF could liquidate at the snap of a finger if the stocks it holds are sufficiently liquid.

Now, I’m not suggesting that WQTM specifically could sell everything at the snap of a finger.

The four quantum plays, although having reasonable market caps now, could turn dicey if the Street were to suddenly abandon the names. But that’s the sort of risk one would naturally have to assume if one wants to invest in the still-embryonic quantum computing field.

As to daily tradability, here again, ETFs aren’t like stocks.

ETFs feature a complex creation-redemption mechanism that constantly allows them to expand and contract the number of outstanding shares as demanded by the marketplace. This is too involved to warrant discussion here, but Schwab gives a clear, plain-English explanation.

The bottom line: Micro-sized ETFs aren’t scary the way micro-sized companies can be. With ETFs, it’s about how risky the stocks it holds are, not how big the overall portfolio is.

Comparing The ETFs

Here is how QTUM and WQTM shape up relative to one another over various time periods. I added, for your reference, the State Street Technology Select SPDR ETF (XLK). I deem the latter a generic tech ETF.

We see here that WQTM, during its short (only since 10/9/25) tenure gave a good account of itself.

It had one notable period of underperformance. Quantum stocks have at times been deeply out of favor on Wall Street. WQTM, with its much bigger genuinely quantum stake, will necessarily reflect that in comparative performance date.

But WQTM will also better reflect bullish quantum moves at times when they occur.

The QTUM-WQTM comparison is not about a performance race. It’s about better reflecting quantum’s rewards and risks (and we can’t have one without the other).

Here are the Technicals.

The quantum-4 (IONQ, RGTI, QBTS, and QUBT) spent much of the period since 10/25 in Wall Street’s doghouse. But lately, they’ve been rallying. WQTM’s price action has been reflecting that.

Risks

As I make clear in my quantum-4 write-ups (see here, here, here and here for the latest), this embryonic field carries high risk.

The ETFs, with their substantial non-quantum exposure, mitigate the risks.

WQTM, as noted, does so in a way that gives investors better opportunities to profit from quantum’s upside.

But WQTM carries some commercial risk. Being so small, it’s hardly likely to be a massive moneymaker for Wisdom Tree.

One wonders whether that firm might decide to pull the plug if the ETF doesn’t gather more assets and generate good fee income.

If that happens — if WQTM liquidates — it would likely sell its positions for 100 cents to the dollar. And then, you’ll see cash appear in your account with a notation about the liquidation. Money-wise, it’ll look as if you decided to sell WQTM.

What to Do About QTUM

I have pending bullish ratings for all of the quantum-4 stocks.

But I won’t carry that through to QTUM. It’s more like a reasonably decent tech ETF than any sort of credible quantum play. That’s enough to preclude a bearish rating.

On the other hand, I’m philosophically opposed to the way QTUM’s portfolio was put together. Also…

I honestly can’t find a way to characterize this overall portfolio. So even when QTUM outperformed XLK, I can’t find a way to discern whether it related to anything systemic, or just plain luck. I’m not into that sort of thing.

So not feeling warranted in being bearish or bullish, I’ll just stay at neutral. Translating that to the Seeking Alpha taxonomy, that means I’m staying at Hold.

data.title: The War Is Not Over
data.description:
Last week, stocks soared for a third consecutive week with the last three days registering new all-time highs for the S&P 500, Nasdaq Composite, and Russell 2000. The Nasdaq has had a 13 session winning streak, which is its longest since 1992. The message the market is sending is that the economic expansion and bull market are both still intact, but the volatility is far from over. The price of crude oil plunged nearly $20 last week with WTI settling at $83.80. The impetus for the rally was news of a 10-day ceasefire followed by reports that the Strait of Hormuz was completely open. In addition, President Trump asserted that the war was over, and that Iran had agreed to suspend its nuclear program and turn over its enriched uranium with a second round of negotiations planned for Monday. That supercharged last week’s rally. Unfortunately, the President’s assertions were overly optimistic and not entirely accurate.

Then the President conditioned the opening of the strait with the proclamation that Iranian vessels would not be able to use the waterway until negotiations resulted in an agreement. This was news to the Iranians, who also indicated that they had never agreed to anything Trump claimed they had. As a result, the regime closed the strait again, indicating it would only be open to those vessels that coordinated with Iran and paid a toll. Regardless, investors bought the major market indexes at all-time highs through Friday. I had my reservations.

Reality struck on Saturday morning when Iran attacked several ships attempting to pass the strait using high-speed gun boats. While their navy may have been largely destroyed, Iran still has shore-based anti-ship missiles, armed drone boats, armed aerial drones, and mines scattered across the waterway.

A second round of negotiations was scheduled for Monday, but Iran’s Supreme National Security Council, which is the decision-making body, stated that it would not engage in a second round of talks because of the Trump administration’s excessive and unrealistic demands. The body also asserted that it will not hand over its enriched uranium, as that is a non-starter to negotiations. This all runs counter to President Trump’s claims last week, which is what stoked the plunge in oil and tremendous rally in risk assets. Despite these rejections, the Trump administration has sent its negotiating team to Pakistan for a meeting on Monday, during which the President claims, Iran will sign an agreement that meets his terms. The quagmire continues, and we are likely to see the price of oil rise and risk asset prices fall to start the week, until the strait is reopened.

I think President Trump is operating under the illusion that the closure of the Strait of Hormuz does not adversely impact the U.S. economy, which he claimed this weekend, but he is woefully mistaken. Perhaps he is just saying this to maintain political capital and perceived leverage in negotiations, but businesses and consumers are already suffering, and the pain is about to get a lot worse. In the latest NFIB small business survey, capital spending plans have fallen to their lowest levels since the Great Financial Crisis in December 2009. Rising energy costs are the culprit here, as business owners say they are being forced to either absorb the costs or pass them on to a weary customer base.

I think we will see a continued softening in consumer spending growth as real income growth starts to decline next month. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) and personal consumption expenditure (PCE) price index are both expected to rise above annualized average hourly earnings growth as early as May. That is not a death knell to the expansion, but it puts us on the warning track should the strait remain closed for several more weeks and keep energy prices elevated. My hope is that terms are reached that end the conflict sooner rather than later with the Trump administration accomplishing some of its goals, but hope is not an investment strategy.

Financial markets are ignoring this risk for now, assuming that the spike in energy costs will be short-lived and not pass through to other goods and services. Investors who were operating under extreme fear just three weeks ago are now becoming greedy in an effort to not be left out of the historic rally in stocks.

While I still believe the major market indexes bottomed three weeks ago just before oil prices peaked, there is not a lot of upside in the market from here in my view until we see more positive developments on the geopolitical front. This is why I have been raising cash in preparation for another inevitable pullback from all-time highs.

During the heat of any geopolitical crisis, fear can consume us and distract from the big picture. Over the past six years, we have had five episodes that you could call crises to varying degrees at the beginning of the year. There was the COVID pandemic, the Russian invasion of Ukraine, the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, the Liberation Day tariff tantrum, and now the war in Iran. If investors had liquidated risk assets at the peak of hysteria during each of these events, they would not have participated in the recoveries. The reason is that the economic fundamentals supporting valuations were still largely intact. Some crises required more stimulus than others, namely the pandemic, but financial markets recovered rapidly each time. I see today as no different, but I am still inclined to raise cash when greed pervades from a tactical standpoint.

At some point, we will have a crisis that is the prelude to much darker days, as was the collapse of Bear Stearns in March 2008, but the economic fundamentals don’t support that currently. Performance year-to-date for different styles and the various segments of the market, as well as international markets, shows the benefits of diversification during this recent drawdown.

This year, I have emphasized small over large and value over growth in terms of stock selection. That is likely to continue once this crisis passes and investors refocus on economic fundamentals. For now, the major market indexes are extremely overbought from a technical perspective, so even without an escalation of the war, we are likely to see a period of consolidation before realizing gains like we have over the past three weeks. Institutions were clearly underallocated to equities leading up to the rebound, which is one reason for the ferocity of the surge in stocks. Short sellers are being forced to cover. There is also a renewed appetite for retail investors to speculate. This is what has taken us from a state of fear to greed. I prefer to sell greed rather than chase momentum.

data.title: Cerebras: Trying To Disrupt Nvidia's Inference Monopoly
data.description:
New Details On Cerebras Systems Ahead Of Its IPO

I wrote about Cerebras Systems (CBRS) in October 2024, when we first saw the initial IPO details, and back then, I called the offering's details quite interesting at the time. For some reason, CBRS didn't go public 2 years ago - the equity capital markets were still in a post-2022 stagnating condition, in my opinion, hence delaying a lot of new issuances. Cerebras withdrew its S-1 in October 2025 despite heightened interest from investors. But now, the situation is changing rapidly, as we saw plenty of AI (and not only AI) tech giants getting ready to list their shares, targeting the end of this year. On April 17th, CBRS filed for IPO again after signing a pair of significant deals with OpenAI (OPENAI) and Amazon (AMZN) Web Services.

I suspect you haven't read my previous article on the stock, so below is a quick description of its business model (from my 2024 research):

The company makes processors for AI training and inference, which are designed to benefit from a growing AI semiconductor market dominated by Nvidia (NVDA). The firm claims to have produced chips "the size of full silicon wafers," and earlier (back in August 2024) unveiled an AI inference solution 20x faster than Nvidia's GPU-based hyperscale clouds.

In late 2024 and 2025, CBRS was getting 87-97% of its revenue from a single customer - the UAE-based AI firm G42. So, you can imagine the overconcentration risk in case they went public at that time. Plus, G42 seems to have close historical ties to China, so it was only adding risks to the whole story. But after signing OpenAI and Amazon, we see Cerebras' risk profile positively shifting. The supply agreement with OpenAI is valued at $20 billion for the next 3 years, and given the fact that OpenAI decided to provide a $1 billion working capital loan to CBRS to fund its data center construction, I assume this agreement will get bigger over time (likely they'll extend it before the initial terms end, given today's rise in compute demand).

What differentiates CBRS from others is that the firm doesn't build small chips that need to be tethered together - their flagship product, WSE-3 (Wafer-Scale Engine 3), is currently the largest commercial AI processor in the world, which was built on a single 300mm silicon wafer. Keeping just one silicon for all the transistors, hundreds of thousands of cores, and memory, Cerebras meaningfully lowers the data transfer latency that chokes traditional GPU clusters. According to the firm's press release from September 2025, the Cerebras CS-3 system proved to be 21x faster than Nvidia's flagship DGX B200 Blackwell GPU:

Given that both OpenAI and Amazon signed deals with CBRS a few months after that release, I assume the superior performance has been validated by these major AI enablers.

So, in my understanding, it's not a story of whether they can build something that competes with NVDA - after the funding from OpenAI (and the two investment rounds we saw since the previous IPO plans withdrawal), the focus is now on execution and timing of the planned expansion.

Execution at a pre-IPO firm is very important to monitor and understand before the company goes public, and CBRS has something to show in this regard. The current CEO, Andrew Feldman, founded Cerebras in 2015, at a time when it was considered physically impossible to develop well-functioning wafer-scale computing because of defect yields and extreme thermal management challenges. But the executive team and mainly the firm's engineering team solved the yield problem by designing an architecture with 100x defect tolerance that dynamically routes around inevitable silicon flaws, achieving a near 100% functional wafer yield, according to the firm's press release.

On the corporate side, execution looks good as well. When the 2024 IPO plans were delayed, management negotiated a restructuring of G42's shares, appeased U.S. regulators to secure CFIUS approval, and then went on to close the largest non-Nvidia AI infrastructure contract in history (with OpenAI and AWS). I think the overall level of execution looks quite solid for such a young company.

Among the main catalysts ahead, I see the benefits of the current CBRS shift from a pure hardware seller to an AI cloud provider. The management announced the rollout of 6 new DCs across the North American region and Europe, aiming to build the industry's largest dedicated AI inference cloud. Some facilities are scheduled to go online between Q2 and Q3 of 2025/2026, and once they do, I believe it's going to add to high-margin recurring cloud revenue ahead.

Another catalyst comes from the AWS deal - CBRS will be deploying its disaggregated inference solution in AWS data centers by combining AWS's Trainium servers with Cerebras CS-3 systems over Elastic Fabric Adapters. I view it as a clear validation step for CBRS - if their tech proves itself on AWS's massive scale, CBRS's offerings will get tempting for those looking for a replacement to Nvidia's chips (at least to some extent).

Valuation Setup Ahead of the IPO

They're likely targeting a May 2026 debut, with reports suggesting a valuation range of $22-25 billion. I think the higher end of that range is more likely because their Series H funding round valued them at a post-money valuation of ~$23 billion (they're unlikely to down-round following the latest deals and corporate developments).

I totally forgot to mention that CBRS had $510 million in revenues for 2025, which was 76% above the previous year. More importantly, they switched from a massive loss (-$485 million) in 2024 to a profitability of $87.9 million as of FY2025, thus proving their business model actually works. The firm's RPO (remaining performance obligations) stood at $24.6 billion as of December 2025, and the figure is likely much higher now. A meaningful portion of that RPO should be recognized throughout the next two years, so forward-looking top-line growth should remain at hypergrowth levels.

The risk here is that CBRS's gross margins stay depressed. The S-1 reveals gross profits of $199.1 million on that $510 million in revenue, implying a gross margin of roughly 39.0% (the hardware-specific margins sit around 36%). If they scale at those margins, the growth in profitability might be in question. But the cloud-related sales will kick in in the next few years, making the GP margins higher, so there's a great chance CBRS will be able to remain profitable (and even increase profitability levels) while scaling further.

At that $510 million in sales, and assuming the firm will get a valuation of $25 billion, the implied P/S ratio is ~49x, which is a lot, when we compare it to NVDA or AMD (AMD):

But assuming that CBRS's sales double this year, and we assess its forward P/S (based on FY2026 figures), the implied FWD P/S should be close to 24.5x, and it's already reasonable when adjusted for the business growth rates beyond 2026.

Your Takeaway

There are still clear risks with CBRS today. The customer concentration risk became smaller, but it didn't go anywhere as the firm essentially traded one dominant master for another (G42 to OpenAI). If the latter partner delays its $10 billion commitment, Cerebras' backlog might evaporate. Plus, they might not find broad-based enterprise hardware demand beyond a handful of hyperscale players; thus, their forward-looking earnings visibility will be damaged. Another risk comes from TSMC (TSM), which is the exclusive partner for Cerebras' Wafer-Scale Engine 3 (built on TSMC's 5nm process). There is no alternative to TSMC right now, so if the Taiwanese giant faces capacity constraints while prioritizing Apple or Nvidia, Cerebras' business scaling plans will be adjusted to the downside.

Despite the risks, Cerebras still looks interesting to me - and it's now even more interesting than at the time of my coverage initiation in 2024 because we see some validation signs for their tech. There's a great chance that CBRS exceeds the $23-25 valuation range when it goes public, and we see a first-day jump like it was the case throughout the post-COVID period. I like the management's execution; the tech itself looks promising, as it adds diversification for Nvidia clients in the coming years. Based on all this, I think CBRS deserves to be rated a Buy ahead of its IPO date.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Immunovant: Best-In-Class Ambitions, Still Unproven
data.description:
Overview

Immunovant, Inc.'s (IMVT) stock has advanced 18.7% since I analyzed it on October 31st.

The company is developing a couple of FcRn inhibitors. The first one is batoclimab, and it is also developing a next-generation candidate, IMVT-1402.

Back in October, the company was rumored to be a takeover candidate. I noted that this wasn't the first time Immunovant was subject to rumors. And I interpreted the most recent rumors as “chatter” rather than an actual leak. But I did admit that M&A activity would not surprise me and that the most likely suitor was Immunovant's own parent company, Roivant Sciences Ltd. (ROIV).

I ended with a “Hold” rating on Immunovant stock, seeing the appeal on both sides, bull and bear.

For me, given the competitive autoimmune market, I want to see patient efficacy data in its 1402 candidate before becoming more bullish.

There have been a few material updates since my last analysis, so below I take another look at Immunovant.

Recent Developments

In February, Immunovant released its third quarter fiscal year 2026 earnings for the period that ended December 31st, 2025. The company had $994.5 million in cash. This came after a $550 million underwritten equity financing in December. So the company effectively extended its cash runway through the potential commercial launch of IMVT-1402 in Graves' disease. Recall that its previous cash runway estimate was just through the 2027 Graves' disease readout.

And it looks like all other IMVT-1402-related milestones are on track. We should see some patient efficacy data later this year.

The news wasn't as bright for Immunovant's lead asset, batoclimab. Announced in April, its Phase 3 Thyroid Eye Disease studies missed their primary efficacy endpoints. Unsurprisingly, the company reiterated its full commitment to IMVT-1402, with Graves' disease as its priority, and that future batoclimab plans would be reviewed with its partner and updated later.

Immunovant moving on from batoclimab would probably be for the best. This is an undifferentiated asset that Immunovant was largely advancing to simply provide insights for IMVT-1402's development. Batoclimab is quite unlikely to compete with the current anti-FcRn leader, argenx SE's (ARGX) Vyvgart. IMVT-1402 was always the focus.

IMVT-1402 has the potential to shake the anti-FcRn market.

Its advantages are threefold:

  • Deeper IgG reduction,
  • Cleaner safety profile on albumin and LDL, and
  • Convenient low-volume subcutaneous self-administration.

Now, Phase 1 data showed IgG reductions as high as 80% in patients taking 600 milligrams every week. Cross-trial limitations aside, this is over 10 percentage points higher than Vyvgart. It is not yet known if further IgG reductions will result in significant incremental benefits, though.

And unlike batoclimab, which lowered albumin and increased LDL, or bad cholesterol, IMVT-1402 has minimal to no impact on both albumin and LDL.

And, although a less important differentiation and something that its competitors could easily reproduce themselves, Immunovant is developing IMVT-1402 as an autoinjector, which is a bit more convenient than having to draw up the drug yourself. I know this firsthand!

Strategy

During the call, management's primary focus was on its lead indication, Graves' disease. It expects pivotal data in 2027 (NCT07018323), with a potential launch slated for late 2028. They called out that deeper IgG suppression is expected to matter for remission.

Difficult-to-treat rheumatoid arthritis is being positioned as the second major revenue opportunity for IMVT-1402. The trial (NCT06754462) is fully enrolled at 170 patients, which is notably 42% above the original 120 target. The company believes this reflected enthusiasm from the patient community, given that this is a tough and pretreated population. Immunovant expects open-label and randomized withdrawal data in the second half of 2026. So, although this is not IMVT-1402's lead indication, this will happen to be the first real test of whether the deeper IgG reductions actually drive incremental clinical benefit.

So, in lieu of human efficacy data, I'm still a bit cautious.

Expectations for IMVT-1402 are very high. In fact, much higher than just delivering the class average efficacy that existing FcRn antibodies already provide. Immunovant's entire investment thesis is built around IMVT-1402 being best in class or first in class in certain indications because of its ability to drive deeper IgG reductions. Therefore, simply matching other therapeutics like Vyvgart in crowded indications will undoubtedly be viewed as underwhelming given Immunovant's current valuation and the “pipeline in a product” narrative.

Difficult-to-treat rheumatoid arthritis is not a “me-too” indication. It's a deliberately chosen high-unmet-need subset. And Immunovant's own rationale is that competitor data already showed that deeper IgG reduction translates into better responses. And of course, IMVT-1402 is designed to go even deeper without the metabolic side effects. And the design of this trial is potentially registrational, but management is on record in signaling that they will only advance aggressively if the data shows something clearly differentiated.

Financial Health

Immunovant ended the year with $994.5 million in cash and equivalents. The company burns roughly $100 million per quarter. Cash used in operations in Q4 was $92.3 million but was over $100 million in the previous four quarters. So this implies that Immunovant has a cash runway of roughly two years. This is consistent with management's own estimate of a cash runway that extends to the planned commercialization of IMVT-1402 in Graves' disease.

Conclusion

Things are looking pretty good for Immunovant, but this all must be viewed within the context of its near-$6 billion market capitalization, which undoubtedly bakes in a lot of success for IMVT-1402. So from an investment perspective, I wouldn't be rushing to buy shares at 52-week highs ahead of critical efficacy data in difficult-to-treat rheumatoid arthritis and Graves' disease.

Essentially, my view of Immunovant as an investment prospect hasn't changed. This is clearly a high-risk, high-reward stock that is likely only appropriate for barbell portfolios (maintain “Hold”).

But recent updates, like the near-total deprioritization of batoclimab, are certainly supportive of its long-term prospects. It's just a matter of IMVT-1402 proving to be actually better than existing FcRn antibodies, which is still very much up for debate.

data.title: NET Power: The Journey Continues
data.description:
NET Power (NPWR) was originally one of those new issues that I thought had a better-than-average chance to succeed. As the last article mentioned, the company is making progress (however bumpy) towards its goal. There is still a cash balance of $379 million left, which is expected to last more than 12 months for sure. In fact, it may last several years. That is probably more important than many things that are being pointed out.

The burn rate for the cash is likely on pause as Baker Hughes (BKR) studies the situation. There will likely be a budget on the cash burn once the study is over with. But right now, it is hard to tell what the commitments are, as everything is being reviewed and likely revised.

We know from the latest annual report that there is enough money to prevent a going concern statement. But until things get going again, that is about all we know until we know what changes (if anything) and what does not.

One of the things about a new idea is that you kind of "wing it" as you go along. So a review period is not unusual. Nor would it be unusual for a variation going forward.

As was covered in previous articles, the company's market value is close to the cash value and short-term investments. That means the business is not valued much. But it has been that way for some time (and several articles).

High Risk

No one should ever consider something like this unless they are prepared for a total loss of their investment. Usually, when I consider something with a risk factor this high, I make a basket, and that basket is restricted to 1% of my portfolio. That way, if one of these falls apart, the loss is easily made up. But if one of these succeeds as expected, then the portfolio has an above-average year. This is definitely not something to "bet the house on" or "go all in on."

Secondly, an idea like this takes several years to work out. There is going to be all kinds of news along the way. In this case there was an impairment of over $1 billion. It is more than possible that original plans have changed, and the accounting reflects that.

One thing about new ideas is that you are "going down a road" that no one traveled before. So, it is not like there is a road map from others that went there before you.

There is probably a chance that the stock "catches fire" from the start because of a story that holds the market's attention. There is also a possibility that the stock later "catches fire" at any step along the way because something happened that entices the market.

In this case, clearly none of the above happened, and the stock has lost most of its value. In many books, including David Dreman's "Contrarian Investment" series of books, the discussion centers upon a 95% chance that the "new issue" stock price will lose 50% or more of its value within the first eighteen months to two years (very roughly). There is also a huge chance that this disappears within the first five years.

Therefore, what has happened should surprise no one. Clearly traders could have had fun with this one (and that is the most likely successful strategy). Some will begin with an extremely small position and then decide whether or not to average down later.

But when this went public, it was clear that this was a development-stage company that was not going to have continuing operations for some years. That appears to still be the case. Basically, we are nowhere close to a conclusion as to whether or not this idea works. Yet clearly the market has a far less enthusiastic view of the potential success than was the case when this went public.

It is also in the 10-K that Baker Hughes wants to study the situation. But that again is something that is very reasonable for the situation, even if the market does not like it.

The key here is that past articles noted companies like Baker Hughes and Occidental Petroleum (OXY) were interested enough in this idea to partner. That does not mean this idea will succeed. But it does give the whole thing a better shot than many similar situations.

Those kinds of partners have a much longer-term view of the situation than does the market. The fact is that no matter the news along the way, the main idea would be that if the company does not liquidate, then the idea still has merit no matter what Mr. Market thinks.

Many do not realize that Occidental Petroleum, Baker Hughes, and many others constantly review new ideas to keep up with changing technology. Out of everything that the company may see or review, only a few make it to this stage. Fewer still will become operating ideas. Even out of that, of the operating ideas, only a few will be worth the effort.

Nonetheless, the "lottery ticket" effect has long attracted investor money to this area. Everyone is looking for that "one" that makes it all worthwhile. This is the same interest that large companies have in watching a startup get to $1 billion or more in revenue. Michael Porter, in his book "Competitive Strategies" will tell you that many large companies are not interested unless there is potentially $1 billion in business available if they enter the market. Then when they do consider entering the market, they often consider acquiring the upstart rather than building the business from scratch.

Note that all of these risks and potential successes are very different from a going concern business. Much of what is going on here takes a whole lot of training (is very technical) in a given area or areas to determine if the idea even has a chance. Everyone involved knows that the chances of success are slim (to say the least). Those chances can change materially up or down as the company proceeds.

Summary

Because of all the risks discussed, it is hard to argue with either a trading strategy or a "wait on the sidelines strategy" when a strong buy idea like this one comes along. This is very much a specialized situation where the total principal of the investment is at risk.

Some would "throw in the towel" when the impairment was announced because the amount was considered significant. Again, that is hard to argue with.

But for those that love this type of investment and have developed a strategy that, on balance, succeeds enough to make the venture worthwhile, the key here is that the company and its partners "have not thrown in the towel." Therefore, there is no conclusion as to whether or not the idea will work out.

That is where the $375 million comes in. Cash burn is always uncertain in a situation like this. But the company will not need financing for at least 12 months. Frankly, in an area like this, that is about the best one can do until next year comes into better focus.

Naturally there are several sub-projects that are going on as well. But probably a major idea that was mentioned in the past is that the first model will be costly. Each one built after that should see material savings for some time to come.

For those unfamiliar with Entropy, please see my coverage of Advantage Energy (AAVVF), as that is yet another development stage company with its own agenda. Advantage Energy updates investors with a corporate presentation most months of the fiscal year. Entropy is a subsidiary of Advantage Energy (with some "name" partners) that may eventually go public.

But we still have several years to go. Now, whether the cash will last "until we get there" is really influenced by the unfolding future. Right now, despite the stock price, this is far from "dead." We really do not know if it was worth the effort "until we get there." But the time and effort that all the partners are putting into this would indicate that there is a reasonable chance of success still even though it is a high-risk situation.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Big Bank Earnings: Resilience And Concern
data.description:
Introduction

Last week, each of the big banks announced their first quarter earnings, among other companies, as the Q1 earnings season got underway. Large bank earnings set the stage for regional banks, which report a week or two later, and influence the banking ETFs. The earnings reports showed that big banks are holding their own through industry headwinds, yet issues such as the private credit market and energy market volatility are weighing on management and investors’ minds.

A Breakdown of Big Bank Earnings

My favorite indicator of how well a bank is performing is looking at its net interest income. Net interest income is the interest income generated by the assets a bank owns, subtracted by the interest expense that the bank pays to borrow funds. While quarter-to-quarter changes are worth monitoring, I find it most useful to look at year-over-year changes when comparing multiple banks.

Regarding the big banks, Citigroup Inc. (C) was far and away the best performer when it came to growing net interest income, with an impressive 12.3% year-over-year growth. Bank of America Corporation (BAC) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) each had 9% net interest income growth, which is a respectable indicator. Wells Fargo & Company's (WFC) NII grew at a rate of 5.2%, which underperformed its big bank peers.

Net interest margin is another indicator that investors should look at when comparing multiple banks’ performance. Net interest margin is a mix of the bank’s average yield on assets and its average yield on borrowing. The formula includes weights for the size of each component.

Net interest margin was tight within 3 basis points for Citigroup, JPMorgan Chase, and Wells Fargo, with a range of 2.47% to 2.50%. Bank of America was 40 basis points lower at 2.07%. Since banks borrow short and lend long (in terms of duration), net interest margin helps show how adaptive banks are to changes in short-term interest rates. With inflationary risks threatening further monetary easing, investors should be careful to watch the effects short-term rate changes may have, specifically on Bank of America.

The final indicator to review is loan performance, specifically how many bad loans are holding back the bank. To accomplish that, I look at the ratio of nonperforming assets to gross loans. This tells me how much of the bank’s primary business (lending) has become non-producing for the banks.

Citigroup and Bank of America each had nonperforming asset ratios of under 50 basis points, demonstrating that each bank was doing a good job with loan performance. At 67 basis points, JPMorgan Chase was higher but still respectable. Even though Wells Fargo came in with the highest NPA ratio at 86 basis points, each bank having an NPA ratio of under 100 basis points suggests all is good with loan performance.

While I would not consider any of the big banks to be in trouble or poor performing, I believe, based on these indicators, that Citigroup is currently the best positioned of the four, with JPMorgan Chase being next, followed by Bank of America and Wells Fargo. Based on this information, I’m expecting Citigroup shares to outperform the other big banks over the next quarter.

Private Credit & Energy Price Concerns

For big banks, the risks tend to mirror the macroeconomic risks. Currently, investors are keeping their eye on the state of the private credit market and the energy price shock. Private credit has been dogged by loan performance concerns, and since the sector is dependent on investor financing (versus deposits), it has struggled to receive (and keep) investment capital. For the big banks, commentary for each of these can be found in the earnings calls for each of the big banks.

In the case of private credit, three out of the four banks disclosed their private credit exposure. Citigroup said it had exposure of $22 billion, with 98% of the portfolio being rated investment grade. JPMorgan Chase said its exposure was approximately $50 billion and that it has ensured diversification by setting sector concentration caps. Wells Fargo did not disclose a specific number but said private credit was a majority of its $36 billion corporate debt finance portfolio.

Bank of America did not disclose its exposure but mentioned it had no material losses, and it is structurally insulated from first loss positions. While the numbers being thrown out by the other three banks sound large, investors need to have a sense of proportion. These four banks carry nearly $4.5 trillion in total loans, making the private credit disclosure minute compared to the size of the overall portfolio.

While the exposure seems to be contained, these disclosures are directly tied to private credit, and because of the way our financial system works, there are likely indirect ties that investors should watch out for. For example, the commercial banking system has lent nearly $2 trillion to institutions that are labelled nondepository. This level of borrowing has tripled in the past five years. It’s possible that these nondepository institutions have various exposures to private credit, which thereby creates additional exposure to the banking system. Only time will tell, but this is an area that investors need to continue to monitor.

Regarding the recent energy price shocks, the big banks also provided commentary that may be helpful to the macroeconomic outlook. JPMorgan Chase stated broadly that higher energy prices increase both market and credit risk but that consumers and small businesses remain highly resilient. Wells Fargo gave specific data, noting that total gas spending edged up a full percentage point higher to 7% of total debit and 5% of total credit card volume, respectively. Wells Fargo also shared a view that I have previously articulated, in that it takes several months for these pricing shocks to go through the broader economy.

Conclusion

Due to their size and scope, big banks provide an excellent source of insight for the broader banking system and our macroeconomic conditions. The first quarter earnings of our largest banks continue to demonstrate the resilience of the economy and consumers in the face of price volatility and credit concerns. While the indirect exposure remains uncertain, I am confident in holding broad bank ETFs to capture the sector’s future upside.

data.title: Grab: Diversification And Value Are Impossible To Ignore
data.description:
Grab (GRAB), the dominant rideshare company in much of Southeast Asia, has been on a tear in recent weeks, recovering from about $3.50 to north of $4.20. The price growth is encouraging, and I don’t believe all of the upside has been tapped into, especially in the long run.

I’ve covered Grab before, and in this article I'll be focusing on things I haven't discussed with much depth. To summarize some key points from my past coverage:

  • Grab is most well-known for rideshare services, but the company is also actively involved in e-commerce and increasingly in financial services.
  • The company is only now shifting into profitability but has enjoyed strong, sustained revenue growth, and various other metrics have performed well.
  • Grab will face stiff competition in many areas, including from well-funded Chinese companies. However, the company already has a dominant position in many markets.
  • Southeast Asia is poised to rank among the strongest growing regions in terms of GDP. Fertility rates are more mixed, but overall, the region is in a better position than Europe, East Asia, and much of the Americas.

As for this update, I believe the valuation, as shown via a simple reverse DCF calculation, is quite favorable. Meanwhile, Grab offers a lot of diversity in terms of geography and business segments. I've mentioned diversification previously but believe it's worth taking a closer look at. Sometimes diversified companies become unfocused, and more ends up being less. However, in Grab’s case, there’s obvious synergy in the business segments.

GRAB: Instant Diversification Across Industries And Nations

I believe diversification is crucial for any stock portfolio. That’s no major revelation, of course, and it’s one of the most basic concepts taught to new investors learning the ropes. Diversifying can be a bit tricky, however, and international investing carries risks, which is why you see Seeking Alpha posting cautionary comments when talking about investments traded overseas. Still, I’d argue that Grab offers one of the easiest ways to quickly diversify pretty much any portfolio.

There’s a tendency to lump Southeast Asian countries into one big amalgamation, but each country is indeed quite distinct. Grab currently operates in 8 different countries. Notably, in Southeast Asia, Grab isn’t available in Laos, Timor-Leste, or Brunei. You will find rideshare providers in these countries, but not Grab. These markets may offer room to grow in the future, but small economies, and in the case of Laos and Timor-Leste, a low GDP per capita, make these markets less attractive.

Regardless, by investing in Grab, you gain access to 8 different economies, several of which are enjoying strong growth. Vietnam, home to more than 100 million people, has been one of the strongest economic performers in the world in recent years. Thailand, meanwhile, is suffering a low fertility rate and stagnant economic growth, but political developments lend hope that improving economic conditions could soon emerge. We could break down the other countries Grab services, and we’ll find plenty of positives, but also caveats. Regardless, by investing in Grab, you can quickly diversify your portfolio in terms of geography. Grab also offers diversification in terms of services and products rendered.

Grab’s bread and butter is rideshare, much like Uber (UBER) and Lyft (LYFT). Revenues from the mobility segment continue to record strong growth. YOY, mobility revenues were up 15% in Q4 compared to a year prior and 16% for the full year. GMV did even better, up 19% for the year. Such strong revenue growth for such an established firm is encouraging. That said, I do wonder how long the company will be able to maintain said growth.

Grab also has a large and popular delivery operation, like DoorDash. Besides hot food, this delivery service is being tied directly into e-commerce. Like Instacart, you can use the Grab app to select products from local merchants. A rider can collect the goods and deliver them to you, often within an hour or less. There are some popular e-commerce platforms across Southeast Asia, including Lazada and Shopee (the largest in many markets). Still, Grab’s large footprint and huge network of drivers should make them a potent competitor in key markets. Both revenues and GMV were up 21% for FY 2025 compared to a year prior.

More recently, Grab has moved into the finance sector, offering loans and payment services. This is an exciting opportunity and one a bit outside of the company’s traditional wheelhouse. Payment solutions and lending could result in profitable new revenue streams and certainly offer diversification from a business perspective. That said, especially with lending, this move strikes me as a higher-risk but high-reward opportunity. The loan portfolio grew by 120% in FY 2025 compared to the prior year, while revenue was up 37% for the year. The rapid growth of the loan portfolio does come with risks, but the generally strong economic growth in many of Grab’s markets should ease pressure.

The connection between rideshare and deliveries is pretty obvious. With financial services, the connection may not be as immediate, but the synergy is still quite strong. One challenge with launching any type of app-based service is simply getting the app on phones. There are numerous payment apps, and building traction is no small task. However, Grab already has more than 50 million monthly active users.

Further, rather than thinking of Grab as a rideshare app, we can think of it as a transaction app. E-commerce and deliveries allow users to get much more value out of the app than just rides. A built-in payment app and loan opportunities can reduce friction, which in turn should fuel engagement with all of Grab’s services rendered.

GRAB Valuation Metrics and Key Fundamental Considerations

Where GRAB really shines right now, in my opinion, is in valuation. Stocks have become quite expensive, especially in the United States. GRAB’s basic metrics may not look all too promising at first glance. The GAAP PE ratio (TTM) is 70.17, for example. The non-GAAP isn’t much better at 64.93. The Price/Sales ratio, however, is pretty solid for a tech company at 5.11. What’s more, as we see with levered free cash flow as illustrated below, GRAB has only recently turned the corner toward profitability. This is perhaps the most crucial aspect. We see this with net income as well, with 2025 being the year when Grab turned profitable.

GRAB Levered Free Cash Flow

GRAB Net Income

The high P/E ratio might scare some investors away, especially if they don’t step back to look at the full picture. Yet a vanilla reverse DCF calculation suggests that GRAB is already fairly valued at its current stock price, and if the company can continue to expand profitability, the current price will look cheap.

GRAB needs to secure levered free cash flow growth of less than 1%, according to my vanilla reverse DCF. This calculation assumed a five-year growth period with the terminal growth rate set at 4% to align with typically observed global GDP growth. I think this is actually a rather ungenerous assumption for a hot growth company like GRAB, and I suspect the high growth runway is higher than 5 years. The discount rate was set at 10% to provide a reasonable hurdle rate for investors to aim for.

Ultimately, the reverse DCF in combination with Grab’s geographic diversity and its burgeoning financial services division results in a buy rating from me. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools provide a hold rating, with valuation and momentum scoring poorly. I tend to downplay momentum as it’s ultimately driven by other factors rather than “self motion.” The valuation grade is rather harsh and, I think, doesn’t fully capture that GRAB has made the profitability pivot.

Why I Could Be Wrong

I am keeping an eye out for competitors. I wouldn’t be surprised if some of China’s large rideshare/delivery platforms try to secure a strong position in Southeast Asia. In the e-commerce space, Shopee is already the top dog in many markets. With financial services, there are a plethora of competitors, and likely the sector will consolidate over time.

Further, the rapid growth of GRAB’s loan portfolio does merit caution. If the economy slows down or inflation accelerates, both of which are quite possible given the Strait of Hormuz closure, people may struggle to repay their loans. At least through the near term, I believe these risks are rather low but must acknowledge that conditions could change rapidly.

Key Takeaway: I’ll Still Grab GRAB

Grab remains a buy, with the valuation via the reverse DCF calculation being a key deciding factor. Strong growth across company segments and diversity in services offered both reinforce my optimism. Lending growth does create some risks but also could open up profitable opportunities.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
